title: Theoretical Foundation of SDDP Convergence
content: The convergence graph illustrates the evolution of the Lower Bounds ($Z_{inf}$) and Upper Bounds ($Z_{sup}$) throughout iterations. The goal is to minimize the gap between these values until a pre-established stopping criterion is met. The Lower Bound ($Z_{inf}$) represents the objective function value at the first stage (Immediate Cost + Expected Future Cost), acting as a floor because the Future Cost Function (FCF) is approximated by Benders cuts. Since the FCF is convex, these cuts are always below or at the same level as the real function: $FCF(x) \ge \sum (\text{Benders Cuts})$.

references.title: SDDP User Manual — Chapter 3: Dispatch Problem Formulation

title: Convergence Criteria: Deterministic vs Stochastic
content: Convergence is verified differently based on the case type. 1. Deterministic Case: Reached when the relative error between bounds is within tolerance: $\frac{Z_{sup} - Z_{inf}}{Z_{sup}} \le \text{Tolerance}$. 2. Stochastic Case: Due to random inflow series, convergence is reached when the Lower Bound ($Z_{inf}$) falls within the 95% confidence interval of the average operating cost calculated in the forward step: $Z_{inf} \in \left[ \bar{Z}_{sup} - 1.96 \frac{\sigma_{Z_{sup}}}{\sqrt{N}}; \bar{Z}_{sup} + 1.96 \frac{\sigma_{Z_{sup}}}{\sqrt{N}} \right]$.

references.title: SDDP User Manual — Chapter 3: Dispatch Problem Formulation

title: Non-Convergence: Insufficient Iterations
content: If the Lower Bound ($Z_{inf}$) shows an approaching trend toward the Upper Bound ($Z_{sup}$) confidence interval in the final stages, but the stopping criterion is not yet met, the defined number of iterations is likely too low for the problem's complexity.

references.title: SDDP User Manual — Chapter 3: Dispatch Problem Formulation

title: Non-Convergence: Convergence Stagnation
content: Stagnation occurs when $Z_{inf}$ stops evolving towards $Z_{sup}$.  Influence of Penalties: Excessive penalty values create cuts with disproportionate slopes, projecting unrealistic values and requiring impractical iterations to reconstruct the function's curvature.

references.title: SDDP User Manual — Chapter 3: Dispatch Problem Formulation

title: Non-Convergence: Convergence Stagnation
content: Stagnation occurs when $Z_{inf}$ stops evolving towards $Z_{sup}$. Sample Size (Forward Series): A low number of forward series limits scenario exploration. Since the maximum number of cuts per iteration is the product of forwards and stages, small samples result in insufficient information to build the Future Cost Function (FCF).

references.title: SDDP User Manual — Chapter 3: Dispatch Problem Formulation

title: Objective Function Analysis: Policy vs. Final Simulation
content: The 'Objective Function: Policy vs. Final Simulation' graph is an essential tool for validating model adherence. It compares the expected value of the objective function obtained in the last iteration of the policy with the final simulation results. If the final simulation yields values outside the policy's confidence interval, it indicates that the operating policy may not be capturing critical system characteristics, resulting in a suboptimal solution.

references.title: SDDP User Manual — Chapter 3: Dispatch Problem Formulation

title: Causes for Deviation: Incomplete Policy and Sampling Discrepancy
content: Two primary reasons for deviations between policy and simulation are: **A. Incomplete Policy:** If the policy phase did not converge, the Future Cost Function (FCF) is not stabilized. The final simulation will use these inaccurate cost signals, invalidating results. **B. Sampling Discrepancy:** If the scenarios in the final simulation differ from those used in the policy calculation (*forwards*), the model may encounter unexplored regions of the problem domain where Benders cuts do not exist, leading to inefficient decisions.

references.title: SDDP User Manual — Chapter 3: Dispatch Problem Formulation

title: Impact of Non-Convexity and Unit Commitment
content: The SDDP algorithm assumes the FCF is convex to generate Benders cuts. However, in hourly simulations, binary variables (like Unit Commitment) introduce non-convexities that the standard policy cannot represent. **Solution:** Enable specific handling in the software via: **Configuration** → **Solution Strategy** → **Policy Non-Convexity**. Select **'Representation of non-convexity in policy'** and define the starting iteration for this logic.

references.title: SDDP User Manual — Chapter 3: Dispatch Problem Formulation
